figures.caption: Figure 7: A pictorial representation of the proof of Proposition D.2. Since h and h′ can be played simultaneously but u and u′ cannot, there must be an infoset like the red dotted one connecting a child of h to a child of h′. Therefore, inflation cannot break existing edges between played nodes.

Table 3: Game sizes of the equivalent representations proposed in the paper (that is, belief game and TB-DAG) on several standard parametric benchmark team games. See Section 8 for a description of the games and for a detailed description of the meaning of each column. Values denoted by ‘—’ are missing due to out-of-time or out-of-memory errors.

Figure 4: An example of imperfect-recall game derived from a team game whose rationale is described in the proof of Theorem 4.11. Given that the terminal values of this game are not relevant, we use z to indicate a generic payoff. The boxes indicated by mini-game and subgame correspond to the terms used in the description.

Figure 2: The connectivity graph for player ▲ and ▼ in the example game from Figure 1. The nodes in the two figures correspond in position, and letter identifiers are added to each node for easier reference. We highlight using the black color the connection between nodes b and c, which is due to the infoset connecting d and e.
Figure 6: A game showing that public state-based approaches do not subsume inflation.
Figure 8: The counterexample for Proposition D.3, for C = 6.

Figure 5: The TB-DAG corresponding to the adversarial team game in Figure 1. Decision and observation nodes are represented in black and white respectively. Note how subgames and terminal nodes that were repeated in the belief game from Figure 3 are now merged into a subgraph which can be reached through different trajectories of play.

Table 4: Runtime of our CFR-based algorithm (column ‘This paper’) using the TB-DAG, compared to the prior state-of-the-art algorithms based on linear programming and column generation by Zhang et al. [59] (‘ZFCS22’), on several standard parametric benchmark games. See Section 8 for a description of the games. Column “u∗” is the TMECor value of the game. Column “Init” represents the time needed to construct the structures needed for solving the games. This corresponds to fully exploring the TB-DAG and computing its full representation in memory in the TB-DAG case. Columns “ε = 10−3” and “ε = 10−4” list the amount of time required for that algorithm to compute an ε-approximate TMECor of the game. Missing or unknown values are denoted by‘—’. For each row, the background color of each runtime column is set proportionally

Figure 3: The belief game constructed by applying MakeBeliefGame to the adversarial team game in Figure 1. To improve clarity, nodes with single actions have been removed. Note how the imperfect recall between nodes b and c from Figure 2 is solved by merging them and using prescription to express an action for each node. Prescriptions have been labeled with the actions prescribed for each node in the belief.
